coredata.dc:title: One pot synthesis of gold – carbon dots nanocomposite and its application for cytosensing of metals for cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelhamid, Hani Nasser
Talib, Abuo
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: Cytosensing of biological transition metals is paramount important for cancer research. One-pot synthesis of the nanocomposite of carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is reported and has been applied for metal cytosensing. The nanocomposite (GCDs) is synthesized using citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine as precursors in the presence of chloroauric acid. The synthesis procedure exhibits many advantages including simple one step, green approach (solvent free method) and requires cheap chemical precursors. The current procedure produces uniform carbon dots (<3nm) and assist the formation of AuNPs without using extra reducing agent. GCDs nanoparticles have UV absorbance matching with the wavelength of N2 laser (337nm). The synergistic effect of the large surface area of GCDs and the maximum absorbance at 337nm offers an effective and promising application for surface enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (SELDI-MS) of biological metals (Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions) for cancer cells. SELDI-MS using GCDs offers a sensitive method, shows selective detection, and provides simultaneous cytosensing of biological metals (Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+) of cancer cells. The metals are detected after complexation with mefenamic acid (MFA) which provides multi-functions (chelating agent, internal calibrant and co-matrix) for LDI-MS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Cytosensing
Metals
Surface enhanced laser desorption
Ionization mass spectrometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Au NPs modified carbon dots (GCDs)
Procedure for cytosensing of metals for cancer cells
Applications of GCDs for laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GCDs using a) TEM, b) EDX analysis and c) elementary contents.

Characterization of AuNPs CDs using a) FTIR, b) UV–vis absorption, inset figure shows the surface plasmonic resonance of AuNPs, c) fluorescence spectroscopy, and d) emission wavelength dependent on excitation wavelength.
Schematic representation of cytosensing of biological metals (Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+) using a) direct method without centrifugation and b) after centrifugation.

Cytosensing of Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ for cancer cells A) without centrifugation and B) after centrifugation of cells a) using MFA and b) AuNPs GCDs in the presence of MFA. Right hand shows the highlighted part of the spectra.
Cytosensing limit of detection for cancer cells without centrifugation, a) 300nmol, b) 200nmol, c) 100nmol, d) 50nmol, and e) 1nmol.

head.ce:title: One pot synthesis of gold – carbon dots nanocomposite and its application for cytosensing of metals for cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Kaohsiung, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung, 80424, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institue of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institue of Medical Science and Technology, National Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica, Kaohsiung, 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors have equal contribution for first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cytosensing of biological transition metals is paramount important for cancer research. One-pot synthesis of the nanocomposite of carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is reported and has been applied for metal cytosensing. The nanocomposite (GCDs) is synthesized using citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine as precursors in the presence of chloroauric acid. The synthesis procedure exhibits many advantages including simple one step, green approach (solvent free method) and requires cheap chemical precursors. The current procedure produces uniform carbon dots (<3nm) and assist the formation of AuNPs without using extra reducing agent. GCDs nanoparticles have UV absorbance matching with the wavelength of N2 laser (337nm). The synergistic effect of the large surface area of GCDs and the maximum absorbance at 337nm offers an effective and promising application for surface enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (SELDI-MS) of biological metals (Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions) for cancer cells. SELDI-MS using GCDs offers a sensitive method, shows selective detection, and provides simultaneous cytosensing of biological metals (Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+) of cancer cells. The metals are detected after complexation with mefenamic acid (MFA) which provides multi-functions (chelating agent, internal calibrant and co-matrix) for LDI-MS.

ce:section.ce:para: Biological transition metals (TMs) play a significant role in living cells. Among those species, copper and iron are important redox-active transition metals in biological cells. Copper (Cu2+) ions, is one of the essential micronutrients and play an important role for the fundamental life processes [1]. It serves as cofactor for proteins and shows an important role in cellular metabolism [1]. Recently (2015), it was found that many cancer cells exhibit an increase of intratumoral copper and/or an altered systemic distribution of copper [1]. In other side, iron (Fe2+, Fe3+) ions are also important metal ions in living cells or organs and play significantly roles in oxygen uptake, oxygen metabolism, and the process of electron transfer [2]. However, excessive iron causes accumulation in body and that lead to tissue damage and organ failure. Thus, the development of effective sensing or detection method for cytosensing of these species is vital and significantly advance clinical, medical and environmental concerns.

Many analytical methods have been reported for the detection of cellular metals (such as K+ and Na+) [3] and transition metals detection for both in vitro and in vivo studies. Transition metals were detected using bioluminescence (BL) that showed selective sensing of Cu2+ ions for in vitro and image of Cu2+ for in vivo [4], electrochemical method [5], fluorescent carbon dots [6], microfluidic platform [7], fluorescence [8,9], and amperometic [10]. Recently (2016), a review article was published for the biosensors of heavy transition metals [11]. These methods showed rapid response time and high sensitivity in detection. But, in most cases, they are expensive, requires a complicated sample preparation and lack of selectivity.

Carbon based nanomaterials, such as heteroatom (N, S, or O) doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or carbon dots (CDs), attracted wide range of scientific and industrial interests. GQDs or CDs is zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures with size below 10nm [12,13]. Carbon nanomaterials were reported for applications such as H2O2 detection in Hela cancer cells [14], metals (Cd2+/Fe3+) in MCF7 cancer cells [15], histidine [16], 2,4,6-trinitrophenol [17], lead ions (Pb2+) [18], Fe3+(using rhodamine B derivative graphene quantum dots) [19]. Carbon dots applications have been reviewed in Reference [20]. The materials have high biocompatibility, small size (below 10nm), easy for modification and possess a promising future. Thus, simple procedure for the synthesis of nanocomposite is important for further applications.

Herein, one pot synthesis of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) modified heteroatoms (N, S, O) doped carbon dots (GCDs) was reported. The procedure is simple, cheap and green technology as it requires no solvent. The presence of heteroatoms such as O, S and N stabilizes Au NPs and prevents aggregation. The material has UV absorption matching with the wavelength of N2 laser (337nm) that enables a promising application for surface enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (SELDI-MS). GCDs offers high cell biocompatability, high sensitivity and unprecedented selectivity for metal ions cytosensing for cancer cells. This method is simple, cheap, offers the capability of simultaneous detection of multi-elements and high throughput analysis.

Citric acid, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), L-cysteine, FeCl3 ·7H2O, FeCl2 ·7H2O, and CuSO4 ·5H2O were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Germany). Mefenamic acid was purchased from Sigma (China). All solutions were prepared using water from Milli-Q Plus water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Jeol, Japan) was used to characterize the prepared material. Simply, colloidal solution (10μL) of GCDs in water was dropped on the thick copper grids (50Å) and wicked away the excessive solution immediately. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philips, CM200, Switzerland) for GCDs was prepared via spotting of about 10μL of the material solution on the carbon films. The fluorescence measurements were investigated using F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co.) equipped with xenon lamp (150W), recorder, dual monochromators and quartz cell. The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on U-3501 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Corporation, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was measured using Perkin Elmer (Spectrum 100). FTIR spectra are recorded from 450cm−1 to 4000cm−1. Laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry time of flight (LDI-MS-TOF) was carried using positive ion and reflectron mode (Microflex, Daltonics Bruker, Bremen) with flight tube, nitrogen laser, pulse width and accelerating potential of 1.25m, 337nm, 3ns and +20kV, respectively. The spectra were obtained using the average of 100–200 laser shots. The laser power was adjusted slightly above the threshold to obtain good resolution and high signal to noise (S/N) ratios.

Cancer cells (A549 cells) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (1:1) and 1% penicillin and incubated at 37°C in humidified incubator with 5% CO2 (Sanyo inc, Japan) for 24h. After scrutinizing the sufficient growth, the cells were treated with trypsin and subculture regularly to 80–90% of confluences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Au NPs modified carbon dots (GCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (500mg) was mixed with cysteine (150mg) and HAuCl4 (75mg). The mixture was heated to 240°C using an oil bath under reflux condition for 3h. During the heat, the solids were melted and the liquid's color was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then to pale brown. The liquid was diluted with ultrapure water (20mL) and HCl (10mL, 0.01molL−1) and then the products were separated using centrifugation (10,000 rmp, 3×30min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for cytosensing of metals for cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Standard solution of Fe2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+(5mmolL−1) were prepared in deionized water. Mefenamic acid solution (100mmolL−1, 50% ethanol and 50% water), and GCDs (5mg/mL) were prepared. The metal solutions (5mL) were added to the cancer cells (1×104 cells/mL). Then, 10μL of the cancer cells was mixed with 10μL of mefenamic acid (MFA) solution. The solution was analyzed and compared with and without GCDs (10μL). The metal ions were detected after separation using centrifugation via the analysis of the supernatant with and without GCDs (10μL). Direct cytosensing of these ions without any separation was also investigated for comparison.

For sensitivity measurement, different concentration (300 nmol, 200 nmol, 100 nmol, 50 nmol, and 1 nmol) of Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions (5mmolL−1) were added to 1mL of the cancer cells and 5mL of MFA solution (100mmolL−1, 50% ethanol and 50% water) as described previously and analyzed with and without GCDs (10μL).

One pot synthesis of Au NPs carbon dots (GCDs) is performed using precursors of chloroauric acid, cysteine and citric acid. CA and cysteine were selected as the carbon source because it can serve as reducing agent and easily form graphene framework through intermolecular dehydration. The procedure is simple, requires cheap chemicals and is a green method because no hazardous chemicals were used or produced during the synthesis. This method requires no solvent and takes place at moderated temperature (240°C). The nanomaterial was further characterized by using TEM ( Fig. 1a), SEM (Fig. 1b), EDX (Fig. 1c), UV–vis absorption ( Fig. 2a), FTIR (Fig. 2b), and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2c–d). Yu and coworkers synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using citric acid (CA) and L-cysteine through a one step hydrothermal treatment (200°C, 3h) [21]. The current approach is fast, solvent free and require no Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1a) clearly shows that the formed GCDs were spherical in shape with size <3nm (average size is 2.8nm). The material was further characterized using SEM (Fig. S1) and EDX (Fig. 1b–c). Data analysis confirms the presence of Au NPs and S, O and N. Presence of these heteroatoms prevent the aggregation of Au NPs. The function groups of the material surface were obtained from FITR spectra (Fig. 2a). FT-IR analysis of GCDs displays the following peaks; stretching vibrations of C–N at 1422cm−1, asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–NH–C at 1125cm−1, bending vibrations of N–H at 1575cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of C˭O at 1678cm−1. Broad absorption bands at 3100–3500cm−1 are assigned as stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. These absorption bands indicate that there are many function groups of amino-containing functional groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. These functions groups stabilize Au NPs and prevent their aggregation. The presence of S–H band groups was confirmed from the observed peak at 2500cm−1. From these findings, it can be easily concluded that the synthesized CDs retain the most characteristic functional group of the reactants, i.e. citric acid, and amino acid (cysteine). Absence of the peak at 890cm−1 indicate that the material is free or have small amount of defect.

To explore the optical properties of the prepared material, UV–vis absorption (Fig. 2b) and photoluminescene emission spectra (Fig. 2c–d) of GCDs were recorded. GCDs aqueous solution has two obvious UV–vis absorbance bands at 280 and 320nm, which can be assigned to π–π* transition of the carbon core [22] and n–π* transition belongs to the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface, respectively [23] (Fig. 2b). Au NPs exhibit weak surface plasmonic resonance absorption (SPR) at 450nm (Fig. 2b). GCDs solution exhibits bright blue luminescence under UV excitation (320–380nm) (Fig. 2c). Under different excitation wavelengths, maximum emission is observed at 340nm (Fig. 2d). These observations indicate that GCDs had an excellent fluorescence property. The emission spectra of GCDs exhibit a typical excitation independent feature as depicted in Fig. 2d. The behavior of excitation wavelength dependent is due to the different emissive sites on GCDs. It could be also due to the different sizes of the prepared material i.e. polydispersed solution of GCDs. The photoluminescence emission (PL) intensity of carbon dots (CDs) isn't fully understood. The emission signal of GCDs belongs to one of the following phenomena: quantum effect, defects and surface states, surface groups, surface passivation, different degrees of π-conjugation, and the recombination of electron–hole pairs localized within small sp2 carbon clusters embedded within sp3 matrix of the emissive trap [24–26]. A comprehensive mechanism for excitation-dependent photoluminescence from graphene quantum dots was recently (2016) published [27]. It is important to mention that the luminescence properties depend on the synthesis procedure and their conditions. Further investigation is highly required to remove this ambiguity.

Fig. 2d shows no change in the emission wavelength upon the excitation with different wavelength (320–380nm). This observation indicates that the emission is mainly due to electron hole recombination. GCDs shows a maximum emission at the absorption wavelength i.e. 340nm. Since there is no shift in the maximum emission peak, we can conclude that the material is homogenous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of GCDs for laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: Laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry using organic matrices [29–31], and inorganic nanomaterials [32–38] is promising and offers many advantages such as high sensitivity, require simple procedure and small volume of the target analyte. New material usually advances LDI-MS application and improve their sensitivity. The synthesized GCDs shows absorption at 337nm that matches with the wavelength of N2 laser of MALDI-MS. This feature reveals that GCDs enhanced laser desorption/ionization process (SELDI-MS) of analyte species. Furthermore, GCDs has large surface area that facilitates the process of desorption/ionization. These features indicate that GCDs enhance the analyte desorption/ionization due to laser absorption as in the case of conventional organic matrix and the large surface area (surface assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry, SALDI-MS). These two characters of GCDs offer a promising application for surface enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (SELDI-MS).

SELDI-MS applications for transition metal cytosensing in cancer cells are simplified as shown in Fig. 3. In order to confirm the applicability of the current approach for metals detection, a standard solution was investigated as shown in Fig. S2. Metals such as Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions form complexes with MFA at 580Da corresponding to [Cu(MFA-H)2(H2O)2+H]+ (Fig. S3). It is important to note that Cu2+ ions complex confirm from the isotope ratio of 63Cu:65Cu (70:30). However, some of these species aren't obvious enough due to the low resolution of these peaks. In other side, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions show peaks at 572 and 580Da that are assigned as [56Fe(MFA-H)2(H2O)2+H]+ and [56Fe(MFA-H)3+H]+, respectively (Fig. S3). These results agree with the previous method that used heavy metal quantum dots (CdS QDs) [39]. Metals (Na+, Ca2+) interactions with MFA take place through the carboxylic groups according to Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) [40]. Mefenamic acid (MFA) was used as a chelating agent for transition metal such as Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. MFA serves as matrix and can be used as internal standard or calibrant for metal cytosensing [41]. MFA is anthranilic acid derivatives (or fenamate) and belongs to the family of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). A comparison between the cytosensing of Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ directly without cell separation (using centrifugation) and after separation is reported as shown in Fig. 3.

Comparison among the cytosensing of Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ simultaneously with ( Fig. 4a) and without separation (Fig. 4b) was performed. MFA shows protonated peaks at 242Da corresponding to [MFA+H]+. It has functions such as carboxylic and amine groups with high binding affinity toward transition metals such as Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions. It was reported that MFA formed complex with Cu2+ at 580Da corresponding to [63Cu(MFA-H)2(H2O)2+H]+ (as shown in Fig. 4). In other side, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions show peaks at 572 and 580Da that are assigned as [56Fe(MFA-H)2(H2O)2+H]+ and [56Fe(MFA-H)3+H]+, respectively (Fig. 4). Chemical structure, chemical formula and mass were plotted in Fig. S3. No obvious signal changes were observed for the metal ions including Ca2+, and Mg2+ ions. It is clear that monovalenet ions such as Na+ or K+ form adducts with MFA. Interestingly, divalent complexes of [Mn(MFA)2(H2O)2], [Co(MFA)2(H2O)2], [Ni(MFA)2(H2O)2], [Cu(MFA)2(H2O)]2, and [Zn(MFA)2] showed the highest antioxidant activity [42]. The formation of complexes with these metals indicate that this approach can be extended to involve these species. These complexes showed anti-proliferative activity in vitro against three human cancer cell lines: MCF-7 (human breast cancer cell line), T24 (bladder cancer cell line), A-549 (non-small cell lung carcinoma) and a mouse fibroblast L-929 cell line [42]. SELDI-MS using GCDs can be used for alkali metals and alkaline earth metal cations such as sodium, potassium, magnesium and calcium [43].

Limit of detection of the target species was determined as shown in Fig. 5. Spectra show that the limit of detection is lower than 1 nmol. It is important to take in consideration that SELDI-MS requires small volume (<5μL). Under the experimental procedure, the data acquisition of the recorded spectra are observed from a tiny portion of the sample spots. It is the part that irradiated by the laser shots. The limit of detection for this method is remarkable. The sampling procedure is simple, fast, requires no expensive instruments. The limit of detection could be improved by cell separation using centrifugation. The current method showed limit of detection lower than 0.5pmol after cell separation as shown in Fig. S4.

GCDs have many advantageous compared to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots such as heavy metal quantum dots e.g. CdS QDs. GCDs have unique optical, photoluminescence properties, are biocompatible, has high water dispersion with remarkable stability, chemical inertness and are environmentally friendly. GCDs has large surface area and display absorption matches with N2 laser of MALDI-MS. Thus, it serves for SELDI-MS application for metal cytosensing of cancer cells. This technology offers multi-elements detection. It can be extended to alkali and alkaline earth metal cations analysis [43]. This method is not affect by pH or phototoxicities of the material [44]. It is an extremely fast method compared to other popular method such as fluorescence spectroscopy that require long time analysis (30min) [45]. It shows no interference effect from the cells that has auto-fluorescence. The current method requires low cost reagents for metals complexation [18]. It can be used as a selective method using chelating reagent that form selective complexation with the target species. This method requires only cheap and non-toxic reagent such as mefenamic acid. Mass spectrometry based technique such as SELDI-MS didn't require expensive recognition molecules or electroactive compound [5]. GCDs has multi-heteroatoms that resulted in dramatic improvement in the electronic characteristics and surface chemical activities [46]. It could be extended for the detection of other ions such as Hg2+ species [47]. GCDs is biocompatible and can be further used for biological applications [48].

We successfully presented one pot method for gold nanoparticles modified carbon dots (GCDs). The synthesis procedure is a green technology and requires no solvent (neat synthesis method). The product is tiny nanoparticle (below 5nm) and has attractive optical properties. The excitation independent emission implies that both the size and the surface state of those GCDs are uniform. GCDs have excellent UV absorption that matches with the wavelength of N2 laser. Thus, it offers high selectivity and good sensitivity to metals cytosensing for cancer cells. GCDs enhanced laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (SELDI-MS) is high throughput, fast, cheap and require no expensive reagents. The current study addressed a strategy for the simple and fast synthesis of uniform GCDs, and expanded the application of the material in the field of nanobiotechnology. Further follow-up work is undertaken and will be reported in the near future for more applications of GCDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the Ministry of science and technology (MOST) of Taiwan. H.N. Abdelhamid thanks Assuit University, Egypt, for the support to carry out this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.11.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
